title: Linear cover time is exponentially unlikely
speaker: Quentin Dubroff
abstract: Proving a 2009 conjecture of Itai Benjamini, we show:  For any C, there is a greater than 0 such that for any simple random walk on an n-vertex graph G, the probability that the first Cn steps of the walk hit every vertex of G is at most exp[-an].  A first ingredient of the proof is a similar statement for Markov chains in which all transition probabilities are less than a suitable function of C.  Joint with Jeff Kahn.

proof_stages.label: Cover-time problem and linear-tail conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines simple random walk and cover time on an n-vertex graph, recalls that expected cover time is at least about n log n, and motivates the conjecture that covering in only cn steps should have probability at most exponentially small in n.

proof_stages.label: Known local cases and the reentry obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Previous results for bounded-degree graphs, trees, and expanders are reviewed, and the key difficulty is identified as controlling how a walk can reenter still-uncovered regions in ways that accelerate covering.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-transition Markov-chain theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main graph theorem is reduced in spirit to a general finite Markov-chain theorem asserting exponential unlikelihood of linear cover time when transition probabilities are uniformly bounded by a small delta, with examples showing why such a nondeterminism condition is needed.

proof_stages.label: Induced-chain smoothing and partition corollary
proof_stages.summary: To handle graph vertices of small degree, the proof passes to induced chains on subsets of vertices, states a robust Markov-chain theorem for hitting a large subset with small incoming probabilities, and derives a partition corollary by applying the theorem to each part and taking a union bound.

proof_stages.label: Finding partitions or proving slowness directly
proof_stages.summary: The graph proof is completed at a high level by showing that suitable large partitions exist under spreading assumptions on the walk, while if those assumptions fail then the walk moves slowly enough that the desired linear-cover tail bound follows by easier means.

proof_stages.label: Hard-to-hit vertices in the regular Markov-chain proof
proof_stages.summary: For the Markov-chain theorem in the high-degree regular case, the proof introduces hitting times H_v, neighborhood-visit times R_v, and events Q_v saying v is hit only after many neighborhood visits, then shows that the set Z of such hard-to-hit vertices has linear expectation and strong concentration throughout the walk.

proof_stages.label: Negative correlation and failure of the naive union bound
proof_stages.summary: A negative-correlation lemma bounds the probability that all Q_v hold on a set I by p^{|I|}, but an unrestricted union bound over all large possible I is too large, so additional structure of the fast-cover event is required.

proof_stages.label: Structural compression and final union bound
proof_stages.summary: Assuming both fast cover and large Z, the proof finds a small set Y' whose neighborhood captures many vertices of Z, enumerates only such structured choices of I, and chooses k and the degree d large enough that this enumeration times the negative-correlation probability is exponentially small.

examples.name: Complete graph coupon-collector cover time
examples.note: The complete graph is worked out by the coupon-collector calculation to show that after cn steps linearly many vertices are still typically missing, motivating the exponential linear-cover conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes the complete graph cover-time scale via coupon collector and then calculates the expected number of missing vertices after cn steps and uses concentration to get an exponential bound.
